coredata.dc:title: Three-cascade cycle amplification strategy for fluorescent detection of HIV-DNA in human blood with hybrid of pentaethylenehexamine-functionalized graphene quantum dot and NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4
coredata.dc:creator: Nana, Li
Ruiyi, Li
Xiulan, Sun
Guangli, Wang
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: Poor water solubility and low efficiency of upconversion luminescence limit the applications of rare earth (RE)-doped upconversion nanoparticles in bioanalysis. The paper reports hybrid of pentaethylenehexamine functionalized graphene quantum dots and NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 via coordination of RE with nitrogen in the presence of polyvinylpyrrolidone. The resulting hybrid offers hexagonal nanostructure with particle size of 25 nm, good dispersibility and rich functional groups. Interestingly, the hybrid promotes the enhancement of upconversion luminescence that depends on the structure of graphene quantum dots. The hybrid was used as fluorescence probe for construction of three-cascade cycle amplification for detection of HIV-DNA. Target DNA interacts with the pre-hybridized duplex DNA to induce release of assistant strand DNA, triggering target DNA-induced recycle. The utilization of three-cascade cycle allows one target DNA to produce many tetramethylrhodamine-labeled DNA fragments. These are connected on the hybrid by complementary base pairs, promoting a significant upconversion fluorescence quenching by fluorescence resonance energy transfer, due to proximity of tetramethyl rhodamine and hybrid. The fluorescence signal decreases with increasing HIV-DNA concentration in the range of 1.0 × 10−17-1.0 × 10-12 M with the detection limit of 4.8 × 10-18 M. The proposed method provides advantage of sensitivity, specificity and stability and was successfully applied in detection of HIV-DNA in human blood.

Nanocomposite
Target-induced DNA recycling amplification
Human immunodeficiency virus gene

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The scheme for hybrid of PEHA-GQD with NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4.
TEM image (A) and HRTEM image (B) of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 and its elemental mappings of Yb (C), Er (D), Gd (E) and N (F).
The XRD patterns (A) of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 and NaGdF4:Yb,Er and FT-IR spectra of PEHA-GQD/ NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4, NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 and PEHA-GQD.
The fluorescence spectra of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4/PVP (a) and NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 (b) under the excitation of 980 nm laser.

The fluorescence spectra (A) and the enhancemet factor (B) and absorption spectra (C) of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 (a), PEHA-GQD-His/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 (b), His-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 (c), GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 (d) and NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 (e).
Fabrication of fluorescence biosensing platform for detection of HIV-DNA.

Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra (A) and fluorescence spectra (B) before (a) and after (b) LDNA was linked to PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4, fluorescence excitation (c) and emission spectrum (d) of TMR (C), and upconversion emission spectrum (e) of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 and ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum (f) of TMR (D).

The upconversion fluorescence spectra (A) of LDNA capped PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 (a) in the reaction systems only lack of Nt.AlwI (b), HIV-DNA (c), TMR-H1 (d), H2 (e) and in the complete reaction system (f) in the presence of 1.0 × 10−13 M HIV-DNA and the fluorescence response value (ΔF) (B) corresponding to different sensing systems.
Effects of H2 concentration (A) and Nt.AlwI cleavage time (B) on the ΔF value caused by 1.0 × 10−13 M of HIV-DNA.

Fluorescence specta (A) of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 in the presence of 1.0 × 10−17, 5.0 × 10−17, 1.0 × 10-16, 5.0 × 10-16, 1.0 × 10-15, 5.0 × 10-15, 1.0 × 10-14, 5.0 × 10-14, 1.0 × 10-13, 5.0 × 10-13 and 1.0 × 10-12 M HIV-DNA (from top to bottom) and the calibration plot (B) between FM value and the logarithm of HIV-DNA concentration.
The ΔF values caused by addition of 1 × 10−13 M HCV, HBV, M-2, M-1 and HIV in the sensing syetem.
DNA sequences of all oligonucleotides used in the experiment.
Comparison of fluorescence methods with different optical probes for detection of HIV-DNA.

head.ce:title: Three-cascade cycle amplification strategy for fluorescent detection of HIV-DNA in human blood with hybrid of pentaethylenehexamine-functionalized graphene quantum dot and NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Poor water solubility and low efficiency of upconversion luminescence limit the applications of rare earth (RE)-doped upconversion nanoparticles in bioanalysis. The paper reports hybrid of pentaethylenehexamine functionalized graphene quantum dots and NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 via coordination of RE with nitrogen in the presence of polyvinylpyrrolidone. The resulting hybrid offers hexagonal nanostructure with particle size of 25 nm, good dispersibility and rich functional groups. Interestingly, the hybrid promotes the enhancement of upconversion luminescence that depends on the structure of graphene quantum dots. The hybrid was used as fluorescence probe for construction of three-cascade cycle amplification for detection of HIV-DNA. Target DNA interacts with the pre-hybridized duplex DNA to induce release of assistant strand DNA, triggering target DNA-induced recycle. The utilization of three-cascade cycle allows one target DNA to produce many tetramethylrhodamine-labeled DNA fragments. These are connected on the hybrid by complementary base pairs, promoting a significant upconversion fluorescence quenching by fluorescence resonance energy transfer, due to proximity of tetramethyl rhodamine and hybrid. The fluorescence signal decreases with increasing HIV-DNA concentration in the range of 1.0 × 10−17-1.0 × 10-12 M with the detection limit of 4.8 × 10-18 M. The proposed method provides advantage of sensitivity, specificity and stability and was successfully applied in detection of HIV-DNA in human blood.

ce:section.ce:para: Rare earth (RE)-doped upconversion nanoparticle (RE-UCNP) may emit high-energy ultraviolet, visible and near-infrared light through multi-photon absorption under low-energy near infrared light excitation [1]. RE-UCNP displays better background fluorescence interference, anti-photobleaching and photostability compared with semiconductor quantum dots and organic fluorescent dyes [2,3]. It has been widely applied in cancer treatment, biological imaging, optical biosensing and photovoltaic equipment [4–7]. However, poor water solubility and low efficiency of upconversion luminescence limit many applications of RE-UCNP in bioanalysis [8,9]. To improve the water-solubility, many strategies were developed for modification of RE-UCNP [10–13]. The most frequently strategy is coating SiO2 on the surface of RE-UCNP. Here, the RE-UCNP was dispersed in tetraethoxylsilane solution and then heated to produce one SiO2 layer on the surface of RE-UCNP via hydrolysis of tetraethoxylsilane. The strategy is simple and environmentally friendly, but the formed UCNP@SiO2 still lacks of enough stability in water and special functional groups for bioanalysis. To resolve the probem, some hydrophilic materials have been investigated for modification of RE-UCNP to improve the dispersivity and functionality, such as hydrophilic polymer [14–16], porous silicon [17], biomolecule [18], protein [19] and DNA [20]. Nevertheless, these modifications not only increase the synthesis difficulty of RE-UCNP, but also may significantly reduce the efficiency of upconversion luminescence. According to the mechanism of upconversion luminescence, the RE-UCNP always has a relatively low efficiency of upconversion luminescence. This restricts the improvement of sensitivity for fluorescent detection of analyte at a low level. To date, great efforts have been devoted to enhance the upconversion luminescence of RE-UCNP. Main techniques include size/phase control [21], doping metal ion [22], matrix modification [23], surface plasma coupled emission [24], core-shell structure [25] and emission dye sensitization [26]. Park and co-workers reported the synthesis of metal/insulator/metal nanoparticle [27]. NaYF4:Yb,Er insulator is sandwiched in the middle of the two layers of gold. The plasma excited by this special structure produce strong local field in the insulator layer, improving the efficiency of upconversion luminescence. Although progress has been made, the synthesis of an ideal RE-UCNP with small partical size, good dispersivity in water, strong upconversion luminescence and rich functional groups still faces a great challenge.

Hybrid of RE-UCNP and graphene has aroused a great interest, because the hybrid may pave one way to improve their optical and chemical properties [28]. Song and co-workers reported graphene oxide (GO)-modified NaYF4:Yb,Er@NaYF4 [29]. The hybrid gives a higher dispersibility, easier surface modification with DNA and better biocompatibility compared to NaYF4:Yb,Er@NaYF4. However, it tends to agglomerate in water due to a relatively large size of GO with a relatively few hydrophilic groups. Compared to GO, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have a much larger specific surface area, more functional groups and better optical properties [30]. As a consequence, the use of GQDs for the modification of RE-UCNP is likely to realize good dispersibility in water and enhancement of upconversion fluorescence. Recently, our group reported several histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (His-GQD) and used for the construction of His-GQD/RE-UCNP. Different from common GQDs, the His-GQD contains many imidazole groups that can coordinate with RE3+ to form stable complex [31]. In the synthesis, His-GQD was reacted with RE3+ to produce corresponding complex. Followed by displacing with F− and hydrothermal treatment. The resulted His-GQD/RE-UCNP offers a small partical size of few nanometers, due to effective regulation of His-GQD on the release rate of RE3+ from the complex. It can be directly dispersed in water, no further modification is needed for bioanalysis. Furthermore, the hybrid of His-GQD and RE-UCNP achieves to an enhanced upconversion fluorescence [32,33]. However, the RE-UCNP prepared by the hydrothermal treatment offers a much lower efficiency of upconversion luminescence compared to the RE-UCNP made by classical solvothermal method.

In the study, we report the hybrid of pentaethylenehexamine functionalized graphene quantum dot (PEHA-GQD) and NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 via coordination of RE with nitrogen in the presence of polyvinyl- pyrrolidone. The as-prepared hybrid possesses hexagonal nanostructure with small partical size, good dispersibility, rich functional groups and strong fluorescence emission. The hybrid has been used for the construction of three-cascade cycle amplification for fluorescent detection of HIV-DNA. The proposed method provides advantage of sensitivity, specificity and stability and was successfully applied in the detection of HIV-DNA in human blood.

The high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)-purified DNA sequences were synthesized by Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Table 1 ). The DNA stock solution was prepared in TE buffer containing 10 mM Tris-HCI, 12.5 mM MgCl2 and 1 mM EDTA at pH 8.0 and stored at −20 °C. PEHA-GQD and NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 were prepared by using the reported methods, respectively [34,35]. The restriction endonuclease (Nt.AlwI) and cutsmart buffer (50 mM potassium acetate, 20 mM Tris-acetate, 10 mM magnesium acetate, 100 μg mL−1 BSA, pH 7.9) were purchased from New England Biolabs, inc (USA). Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and other reagents were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd (Mainland, China). The phosphate-buffered solution (PBS) (Na2HPO4-NaH2PO4-NaCl, 0.1 M PBS, pH 7.0) and MES buffer (0.1 M MES, 0.5 M NaCl, pH = 6) were prepared in the laboratory. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used purified by the Milli-Q purification system in the whole experiment.

The ligand-free NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 (4 mg) prepared by using the same procedure reported in literature [36] was dispersed in ethanol of 5 mL. Added 5 mL of the ethanol solution containing 0.3 g PVP into and stirred for 2 h. Followed by centrifugation at 15,000 rpm and washing with ethanol for three times to obtain solid NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4/PVP. The collected solid product was redispersed in ultrapure water of 5 mL with the help of high frequency ultrasound (46 KHz) and then mixed with the PEHA-GQD aqueous solution (0.4 mg mL −1, 4 mL). After stirred for 30 min, the mixed solution was centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 20 min and washed with ultrapure water three times. The collected PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 was redispersed in ultrapure water of 5 mL with the help of high frequency ultrasound (46 KHz) and stored at 4 °C before use.

The LDNA was covalently connected the surface of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 via carbodiimide reaction between carboxy-terminated LDNA and amino functionalized PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 [37]. Typically, EDC (20 mg mL−1, 50 μL, MES buffer) and NHS (20 mg mL−1, 50 μL, MES buffer) were added to a LDNA solution (10 μM, 50 μL), and incubated at 37 °C for 90 min to activate the carboxyl groups on LDNA. Then the PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 dispersion was added to the solution and vibrated at 4 °C overnight. After that, the solution was ultrafiltered using a Vivaspin concentrator (Sartorius, 10,000 MW) at 3000 rpm and 4 °C for 15 min to remove excess LDNA. The ultrafiltration was performed twice with PBS buffer. The obtained LDNA-capped PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 solution was stored at 4 °C.

Before use, the AS, AP, TMR-H1 and H2 were separately heated at the temperature of 95 °C for 5 min and slowly cooled down to 37 °C. Equal volumes of the pre-treated AS (1 μM) and AP (1 μM) solutions were mixed and incubated for 2 h at 37 °C. Then, its 5 μL and the HIV-DNA standard solution or sample solution (25 μL) were added into a centrifuge tube of 1.5 mL. The mixed solution was incubated for 2 h at 37 °C and then added the H1 (10 μM, 5 μL), H2 (10 μM, 5 μL) and cutsmart buffer (5 μL). After incubated for 45 min at 37 °C, the Nt.AlwI solution (2 U μL−1, 5 μL) was added, the mixed solution was incubated for 1 h at 37 °C. Afterwards, the Nt.AlwI was deactivated at 80 °C for 20 min to terminate the reaction. It was treated by adding the mixed solution of LDNA-capped PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 dispersion (400 μL) with the CS solution (1 μM, 50 μL), shaking well and incubated at 37 °C for 90 min. Its upconversion fluorescence was measured on the fluorescence spectrometer with an external 980 nm laser as the excitation light source and the slit width was 2 nm.

The hybrid of PEHA-GQD with NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 includes two cumulative steps (Fig. 1 ). Firstly, the ligand-free NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 is combined with PVP in ethanol under stirring to form NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4/PVP composite. Due to special amphiphilic structure, PVP molecules are easily adsorbed on the hydrophobic surface of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. The existence of hydrophilic shell helps to improve the dispersivity of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 in ethanol. The result shows that in the absence of PVP the NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 quickly precipitates from ethanol when the stirring stops. In the presence of PVP, the combination results in the formation of an opalescent dispersion. With the increase of PVP conentration the stabilization time of the dispersion will be longer. However, the stabilization time can not be further improved by increasing the concentration of PVP, when the addition of PVP in ethanol of 5 mL is more than 0.3 g. This is mainly due to the equilibrium of PVP adsorption on the surface of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. Next, the NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4/PVP aqueous solution was mixed with the PEHA-GQD aqueous solution to produce the PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 hybrid. Owing to small sheets of graphene with the rich of hydrophilic groups, PEHA-GQD is easy to migrate to the surface of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4/PVP and produce a strong interaction because of their strong polarity. Furthermore, PEHA-GQD contains many amino and imino groups. The nitrogen atoms in these functional groups coordinate with RE3+ on the surface of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. The coordination achieves to the hybrid of PEHA-GQD and NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. The covalent connection is beneficial to improve the stability of formed hybrid, enhancing the efficiency of energy transfer between PEHA-GQD and NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4.

The as-synthesized hybrid was characterized by TEM, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), two-dimensional elemental mapping, XRD and FT-IR spectrum. Fig. 2 presents the TEM, HRTEM and elemental mappings of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. The NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 possesses the hexagonal structure with an average size of about 25 nm. There are two lattice fringes on the HRTEM image. The lattice spacings of 0.51 nm and 0.2 nm are corresponded to the (100) crystal surface of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 and the (100) crystal surface of graphene, respectively. The result of elemental mapping analysis proves that the hybrid contains Gd, Yb, Er and N elements. As Gd, Yb and Er elements only exist in the NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 and N element only includes in the PEHA-GQD, the above result verifies that our synthesis achieves to the hybrid of PEHA-GQD and NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. Fig. 3 presents the XRD patterns of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 and NaGdF4:Yb,Er as well as FT-IR spectra of PEHA-GQD and PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. It can be seen from Fig. 3 that the core-shell NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 has a similar XRD pattern with NaGdF4:Yb,Er. The each material offers the (100), (110), (101), (210) and (102) crystal faces, which are consistent with the XRD pattern of hexagonal phase NaGdF4 (JCPDS No. 27-0699). However, NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 has a higher crystallinity compared to NaGdF4:Yb,Er. There are many absorption peaks and bands on the FT-IR spectrum of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. The wide IR absorption peak at the wavenumber from 3500 cm−1 to 3000 cm−1 is caused by stretching vibration of OH and NH. The CO stretching vibration of amide has IR absorption peak at 1650 cm−1. The strong IR absorption peak at 1550 cm−1 is attributed to bending vibration in the NH bond plane of primary amine as well as the asymmetric stretching vibration of CO bond. In the range of 1700−700 cm−1, the absorption peaks may be attributed to functional groups in pentaethylenehexamine. The absorption peak near 1000 cm−1 is attributed to strong absorption of Yb3+ at 980 nm. All IR absorption peaks and bands of PEHA-GQD can be found from the IR spectrum of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4, verifying that PEHA-GQD was successfully attached to the surface of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4.

To examine the effect of modification on the efficiency of upconversion fluorescence, the fluorescence behaviours of different materials were investigated. Fig. 4 shows that the intensity of upconversion fluorescence of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4/PVP is higher than that of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. The maximum luminescence intensity of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4/PVP is more than 1.4 fold that of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4, verifying that the introduction of PVP enhances the upconversion luminescence. This is because PVP contains the rich of oxygen and nitrogen atoms. They as the electron donor improve the energy transfer and results in the enhancement of upconversion fluorescence. Of course, the existence of PVP layer partially blocks the absorption of 980 nm excitation by NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 and reduce the efficiency of upconversion fluorescence. However, the influence of this factor on the fluorescence is very limited because of the PVP layer is very thin.

Fig. 5 shows the upconversion fluorescence spectra and enhancement factors of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 modified by different graphene quantum dots. All materials display a similar upconversion fluorescence spectrum, verifying that all GQDs used cannot change the energy level of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 for upconversion luminescence. However, the hybrid with GQDs produces an enhanced upconversion fluorescence. Interestingly, the enhancement depends on the structure of GQDs. The fluorescence intensity of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 is more than 1.8 fold that of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4, verifying that the introduction of PEHA-GQD remarkably enhances the efficiency of upconversion fluorescence of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. This is because PEHA-GQD can bind with NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 by coordination of amino and imino in PEHA-GQD with RE3+ on the surface of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. Histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (His-GQD) consist of small sheets of graphene rich in imidazole groups. These imidazole groups can combine with the RE3+ via the coordination bond. This helps to realize the covalent combination between His-GQD and NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. This closer integration is more conducive to accelerating the energy transfer between His-GQD and NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4, improving the enhancement of upconversion fluorescence. Common graphene quantum dots from pyrolysis of citric acid (GQD) brings the minimal enhancement of upconversion fluorescence. Although the GQD has good water-solubility, it lacks of the functional groups that can covalently combine with NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. Consequently, the GQDs are easy to fall off from the surface of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. This reduces the enhancement of upconversion fluorescence. Among four kinds of graphene quantum dots, the pentaethylenehexamine and histidine co-functionalized graphene quantum dot (PEHA-GQD-His) produces the highest enhancement of upconversion fluorescence. This is due to functional integration of imidazole, amino and imino. However, we observed some precipitate in after the PEHA-GQD-His/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 aqueous solution was placed at room temperature for 4 h. Compared to PEHA-GQD-His/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4, PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 has a much better water-solubility. The PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 aqueous solution can be stable for more than 3 months. In order to obtain high stability and enhancement of upconversion fluorescence, PEHA-GQD was used for NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 modification.

To understand the enhancement mechanism, the absorption spectra of various graphene quantum dots were measured in the near infrared region. Fig. 5C shows that the each of four GQDs has an obvious absorption peak near 980 nm. The result indicates that all GQDs have strong photon absorption capacity towards near infrared light. However, the capacity seriously depends on the structure of GQDs. The absorption peak of His-GQD at the near infrared region is higher than that of GQD. This is because the introduction of histidine enlarges the electron conjugation system of GQD. This helps to improve the absorption capacity of GQD towards near infrared light. When GQD was replaced by PEHA-GQD, the near infrared absorption was further enhanced. This is because the existence of electron donating amino and imino in the PEHA-GQD accelerates the energy transfer between PEHA-GQD and NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. Among four kinds of GQDs, PEHA-GQD-His gives a higher near infrared absorption, due to the integration of above factors. Based on the above results, we suggest that PEHA-GQD acts as an antenna for absorbing near infrared light and produce enhanced upconversion fluorescence. Under near infrared radiation of 980 nm laser, PEHA-GQD efficiently absorbs the near infrared light and transfer the NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 quickly. This improves the absorption capacity of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 towards the near infrared light and results in the enhancement of upconversion fluorescence efficiency. The GQDs with a higher absorption capacity of near infrared light and a faster energy transfer brings a higher enhancement of upconversion fluorescence. The enhancement depends on rapid energy transfer between GQDs and NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. In order to understand the energy transfer mechanism, the fluorescence emission spectrum of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 and ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum of PEHA-GQD were measured. Fig. S1 indicates that the absorption band of PEHA-GQD has obvious spectral overlap with the emission spectrum of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. The result demonstrates that the fluorescence enhancement is mainly attributed to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [9]. Furthermore, the covalent connections greatly shorten the distance between PEHA-GQD and NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 in the hybrid. This further enhances the efficiency of upconversion fluorescence due to an improved energy transfer efficiency.

PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 was designed as a fluorescence probe for detection of HIV-DNA coupled with three-cascade cycle amplification (Fig. 6 ). In the detection, the aptamer (AP) is firstly combined with the assistant strand (AS) to form duplex DNA (AP-AS). In the presence of target DNA, target DNA specifically binds with the AP in AP-AS duplex. This results in dehybridizes of AP-AS and releases of one free AS. The released AS combines with the pre-designed region in hairpin TMR-H1 to form double-stranded DNA (TMR-H1-AS) with one restriction endonuclease Nt.AlwI recognition site. Subsequently, Nt.AlwI cleavages TMR-H1-AS at the recognition site. In the process, TMR-H1 was selectively cleave into two DNA fragments (BS and FS). The AS will again interact with the un-nicked hairpin TMR-H1 to initiate the recycling process, producting a large number of BS and FS (Cycle I). The formed BS will specifically bind with H2 to form H2-BS duplex with a Nt.AlwI recognition site. This results in the initiation of another cleavage cycle of H2 (Cycle II) to release the DNA fragment BS and AS. The released AS in the cycle II triggers the cyclic cleaveage of TMR-H1 and initiate the new recycling process (Cycle III). By the three-cascade recycle, one target HIV-DNA produces many DNA fragments FS. This leads to a significant signal amplification.

LDNA was covalently connected the surface of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 via carbodiimide reaction between carboxyl group in LDNA and amino group in PEHA-GQD. Fig. 7 A and B show the ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra before and after LDNA was linked to PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. On the absorption spectrum of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 there is an absorption peak at near 350 nm. The peak is due to the ultraviolet-visible absorption of PEHA-GQD in the hybrid. This demonstrates that PEHA-GQD has been successfully connected to the surface of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 again. After LDNA was linked, one new absorption peak at 260 nm appears in the absorption spectrum. The result verifies that LDNA was successfully connected to the surface of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. Moreover, the LDNA connection only brings a slightly reduce in the fluorescence intensity. This is owe to the fluorescence quenching caused by the non-specific binding of LDNA molecules on the surface of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. Then, the formed FS hybridizes with the PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 by base complementary pairs. The TMR in FS shows the maximum excitation peak at 550 nm and emission peak at 580 nm (Fig. 7C). Because the ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum of TMR and fluorescence spectrum of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 seriously overlap, the above hybridization results in an obvious upconversion fluorescence quenching process by FRET between TMR and PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. The fluorescence emission of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 at 542 nm was also used as an excitation light source of TMR, which transfers the part energy of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 to TMR. This leads to further reduction in the upconversion luminescence intensity at 542 nm and increase in the fluorescence of TMR at 580 nm. Therefore, our sensing platform can be applied to fluorescence determination of HIV-DNA with high sensitivity and specificity.

To evaluate the role of various reagents in the cycle amplification reaction, the fluorescence performance of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 was investigated in different reaction systems. Fig. 8 shows that in the reaction system without Nt.AlwI, target HIV-DNA or TMR-H1, the change in fluorescence intensity (ΔF) is excellent small. In the absence of Nt.AlwI, HIV-DNA or TMR-H1, the three-cascade cycle amplification reaction, including cycle I, cycle II and cycle III, cannot be triggered. No FS was produced after the detection reaction, thus the fluorescnece response remains stable throughout. However, the change will obviously increase in the reaction system without H2. This is because in the presence of TMR-H1, the cycle I can be realized. By the cycle I, one target DNA produces many FS fragments. This leads to an increased change in fluorescence intensity of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. Compared with the above reaction systems, the complete reaction system causes the maximum change in luminescence intensity of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. In the complete reaction system, the three-cascade cycle reaction can be triggered. By the target-induced DNA recycling, one target DNA produces many FS. The formed FS hybridizes with PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 and results in the fluoresence quenching. This produces a significant signal amplification of biosensing system.

To optmize the conditions for fluorescent detection of HIV-DNA, the fluorescence response (ΔF) towards 1.0 × 10−13 M HIV-DNA was investigated by using the same method described in the Section 2.4 unless varying one reaction condition. Fig. 9 A indicates the ΔF values at different H2 concentration. It can be seen from Fig. 9A that the ΔF value will rapidly increase with the increase of H2 concentration when H2 concentration is less than 10 μM. This is because the number of repeatable cycle II and cycle III increases in the presence of a relatively high H2 concentration. This produces more DNA fragments FS. These hybridize with PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 to produce a more fluorescence quenching, indicating an increased ΔF value. However, the ΔF value reaches its maximum and then tends to constant when the H2 concentration is higher than 10 μM. With the increase of H2 concentration, more and more H1 was cleave into DNA fragment FS, but the total amount of final cleavage product FS was constant. Moreover, with the reaction progresses, a large number of DNA fragments FS are connected to PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4, and the steric resistance on its surface increases, making it difficult for the redundant FS to approach NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4/PEHA-GQD. Therefore, the ΔF value of NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4/PEHA-GQD no longer increases with the increase of H2 concentration when H2 concentration is more than 10 μM. To obtain a high sensitivity, the H2 concentration of 10 μM was applied to the fluorescence quantitative determination of HIV-DNA.

The ΔF values at the different Nt.AlwI cleavage times were presented at Fig. 9B. When the cleavage time is less than 60 min, the ΔF value will increase with the increase of cleavage time. With increase of cleavage time, more TMR-H1-As duplexes are cleavaged and produced more FS, BS and AS. This accelerates the three-cascade cycle reaction, finally leading to an enhanced ΔF value. However, the ΔF value reaches its maximum and then tends to constant when the cleavage time is more than 60 min. This is because the H1 concentration was fixed in the sensing system. With the increase of the cleavage time, the H1 concentration will gradually decrease in the reaction system. When the cleavage time exceeds 60 min, the three-cascade cycle amplification reaction is close to its equilibrium state. Thus, number of the formed DNA fragments FS will no longer obviously increase with the increasing time. As a result, the ΔF value of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 remains stable. To obtain high sensitivity and save reaction time, the cleavage time of 60 min was applied to fluorescence detection of HIV-DNA. Based on the above method, we further optimized the concentration of other reagents in the poposed sensing system, including Nt. AlwI, AP, AS, H1, CS and PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. The optimized concentrations are 2 U μL−1 of Nt.AlwI, 1 μM of AP, 1 μM of AS, 10 μM of H1, 1 μM of CS and 0.8 mg mL−1 of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4.

To verify the feasibility of the sensing platform for fluorescent determination of HIV-DNA, the upconversion fluorescence performance of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 with different HIV-DNA concentration was studied. Fig. 10 A indicates the intensity of upconversion fluorescence will decrease with the increase of HIV-DNA concentration. The result demonstrates that the fluorescence response depends on the HIV-DNA concentration. In addition, we also observe that with the increase of HIV-DNA concentration the fluorescence intensity of TMR increases (Fig. S2). This is because a higher HIV-DNA concentration brings more TMR fluorescence probes to the surface of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. The interaction between TMR and PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 leads to an enhanced fluorescence intensity of TMR, because the energy transfer of PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 to TMR. Further, Fig. 10B shows the calibration plot between the maximum value of luminescence intensity FM and the logarithm of concentration. The FM has a good linear relationship with the logarithm of HIV-DNA concentration in the range of 1.0 × 10−17 to 1.0 × 10-12 M. The corresponding linear regression equation is FM =-2257.8×LOG[CHIV-DNA,M]-15932, and the correlation coefficient of 0.995. The detection limit was 4.5 × 10-18 M based on 3σ rule. The sensitivity is better than that of previously reported biosensing system for fluorescence determination of HIV-DNA in Table 2 . In order to evaluate the effect of PEHA-GQD on the sensitivity, GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 and His-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 were used to replace the PEHA-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 in the sensing platform for fluorescent detection of HIV-DNA, respectively. The results indicate that the detection limits were 6.2 × 10−17 M with GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 and 2.1 × 10-18 M with His-GQD/NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. This confirms that the hybrid of PEHA-GQD and NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4 can improve the sensitivity for fluorescent detection of HIV-DNA.

The repeatability of the proposed method was verified by measuring fifty identical sample solutions with HIV-DNA concentration of 5.0 × 10−13 M. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the experimental results is 2.46 %, which has satisfactory repeatability.

For the detection of HIV-DNA, the main interference should come from the coexisting DNA species in human blood. Some DNA sequences were selected for evaluating the specificity of proposed method, including hepatitis B virus (HBV), hepatitis C virus (HCV), double-base mismatched DNA strand (M-2) and single base mismatched DNA strand (M-1). Fig. 11 shows that only HIV-DNA can cause a significant fluorescence response among the DNA sequences studied, indicating an excellent specificity. This is attribute to a special DNA sequence design of AS and AP. HIV-DNA can hybridize with AS-AP because the number of complementary bases to AP in HIV-DNA is more than that in AS to AP. Thus, the hybridization of HIV-DNA with AS-AP results in the release of AS from AS-AP duplex. The released AS triggers the three-cascade cycle reaction and produces a significant fluorescence response. As the number of complementary bases to AP in HCV and HBV are very small, both HCV and HBC cannot efficiently open double-stranded structure of AS-AP. Due to lack of free AS, three-cascade cycle amplification cannot be achieved. Thus, the existence of HCV or HBV doesn‘t bring an obviously increased ΔF value. Different from HCV and HBV, the base sequences of M-1 and M-2 are highly similar to that of HIV-DNA. The characteristic determines that they are most likely to interfere with the detection of HIV-DNA. However, Fig. 10 shows that M-1 and M-2 produce only a small signal change. In addition, the upconversion fluorescence measurement can effectively avoid background fluorescence interference from protein and other biological macromolecules in human blood. The proposed analytical method can be used for detection of HIV-DNA in human blood.

To evaluate the potential application of the fluorescent biosensing system in real sample analysis. the known concentration of HIV-DNA standard solution was added to human blood samples for spike recovery experiments. Firstly, the blood samples were thrawed at room temperature and mixed well. Then, the upconversion fluorescence assay were measured in diluted human blood samples. Add the known standard concentration of HIV-DNA to diluted blood samples and follow the same detection procedure as buffer solution. Table 3 shows the recovery of HIV-DNA in the blood samples were in the range of 93.5–106 %. The results of the proposed method are consistent with those obtained by the electrochemical method [43], verifying that the proposed method has good accuracy.

The study demonstrates the hybrid of pentaethylenehexamine functional graphene quantum dots and NaGdF4:Yb,Er@NaGdF4. The hybrid displays good dispersibility in water, rich functional groups and sigificant upconversion luminescence enhancement. The hybrids was used as optical probes to construct the fluorescent biosensing platform for detecting HIV-DNA based on three-cascade cycle amplification strategy. The analytical method provides the advantages of sensitivity, selectivity and repeatability. It can be widely applied in highly sensitive detection of DNA in clinical samples.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Li Nana is currently a Doctor candidate in School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. Her research interest is synthesis of graphene nanohybrids and their application in optical sensors.

Li Ruiyi is currently an associate professor in School of School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Jiangnan University, China. She received his master degree from The Birmingham University, The United Kingdom, 2014. Her research interest is synthesis of graphene-based nanomaterials and their application in electrochemical sensors.

Sun Xiulan is a professor of School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, China. She received his Doctor degree from Jiangnan University, China, in 2006. She is author of more than 100 papers. She current research interests are food saftey.

Wang Guangli is a professor of School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. She received his doctor degree from Nanjing University, China, in 2009. She is author of more than 100 papers and reviewer of more than 10 journals. Her current research interests are synthesis of nanomaterials and their applications in analytical chemistry.

Li Zaijun is a professor of School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. He received his doctor degree from Jiangnan University, China, in 2004. He is author of more than 300 papers and reviewer of more than 40 journals. His current research interests are synthesis of graphene-based nanomaterials and their applications in sensing, electrocatalysis and energy storage/conversion devices.
